note: DELTA Deck scouting reports. Grounded in: Start Profile (league-invariant pos|fmt thresholds, corrected +0.5 TE premium), PPG/EPA arcs, contracts, market position ranks. Deliberately excludes live buy/sell verdicts and exact market values (drift nightly). REGENERATE when scoring, thresholds, or season data change.

reports.Josh Allen: Allen hits in 76% of starts with a 26% elite rate across 34 games — still the position's most reliable weekly floor, locked through 2030 on a generational deal. The concern is direction: PPG has slid three straight years (24.2→22.7→22.0) and EPA/play fell from 0.31 to 0.17, so you're paying QB1-by-market price for a profile entering the back half of its peak at 29.8.
reports.Drake Maye: Maye's leap is real — 14.4 to 21.1 PPG with EPA/play exploding from -0.03 to 0.26, and a 77% hit rate over 30 games. The 7% elite rate says the ceiling games haven't arrived yet; at 23.6 and locked through 2028, he's a foundation piece whose best football is plausibly ahead.
reports.Jayden Daniels: Daniels regressed from 21.5 to 16.7 PPG and his EPA/play went negative (-0.01) in year two, a genuine warning sign. The profile still shows a 75% hit rate with 25% elite across 24 games, and at 25.2 the talent case is intact — but the sophomore tape demands the 2026 season answer questions.
reports.Caleb Williams: Williams improved from 15.3 to 19.0 PPG and flipped his EPA/play positive (-0.06→0.07), but the weekly profile is still rough: 44% misses with only 9% elite across 34 games. He's a volume of starts away from proving the leap is durable — the arrow points up, the floor doesn't yet.
reports.Lamar Jackson: Lamar misses just 17% of starts — the best floor in the QB room — with 23% elite across 30 games. The flags: 2025 cratered to 17.1 PPG from 25.6, EPA/play collapsed 0.28→0.07, and at 29.2 with a contract through 2027, the window math is starting to matter.
reports.Patrick Mahomes: The brand outruns the profile: 37% misses with only a 13% elite rate over 30 games — he's been the most mediocre version of himself for stretches of two years. PPG did rebound to 21.1 and the contract runs through 2031, but the weekly output says volatile mid-QB1, not the old inevitability.
reports.Jalen Hurts: Hurts is quietly steady — 81% hit rate, only 19% misses across 31 games — but the trend is gentle erosion: 21.9→21.3→19.1 PPG with EPA/play halving to 0.07. At 27.6 and locked through 2028, he's a stable asset whose rushing-driven floor is doing more of the work each year.
reports.Justin Herbert: Herbert's profile keeps refusing to match the arm: 39% misses, 15% elite across 33 games, with EPA/play stuck at 0.03. The 18.7 PPG season was fine, but eight years in, 'fine' is the pattern — locked through 2029 as a high-floor name whose ceiling weeks stay rare.
reports.Bo Nix: Nix has stacked two nearly identical seasons (19.3, 18.6 PPG) with a 65% hit rate and 15% elite across a full 34 games. That's a dependable QB2-plus profile on a rookie deal through 2028 — what's missing is any sign of a third gear.
reports.Trevor Lawrence: Lawrence finally produced — 20.6 PPG, a career year — and the 70% hit rate over 27 games is respectable. But a 7% elite rate exposes the issue: he wins on accumulation, not eruption, and the EPA arc (0.09 in 2025) says good, not special. Locked through 2030.
reports.Jared Goff: Goff is a metronome: 17-20 PPG three years running, 65% hit rate, with a 2025 EPA/play of 0.14 that's quietly strong. The 9% elite rate caps the fantasy appeal — he rarely wins a week by himself — but at QB through 2028 in Detroit, the floor is bankable.
reports.Dak Prescott: Dak rebounded to 19.0 PPG with EPA/play back to 0.14, but the profile shows 40% misses across just 25 games — availability and consistency are both wobbling at 32.6. Locked through 2028, he's a serviceable bridge whose elite weeks (16%) still flash occasionally.
reports.Brock Purdy: Purdy posted a career-best 20.8 PPG with a 0.23 EPA/play, and his 71% hit rate over 24 games keeps the efficiency narrative alive. The 13% elite rate is modest for the production level — the system does some lifting — but locked through 2030 at 26.2, the profile is firmly in the QB1 conversation.
reports.Jordan Love: Love's profile has stalled: 40% misses, just 7% elite across 30 games, with PPG flat at 16 for two straight seasons. The 0.24 EPA/play from 2025 hints the underlying play is better than the box scores, but three years in, the boom weeks haven't materialized.
reports.Baker Mayfield: Baker fell back to 16.6 PPG from the 22.5 outlier season, and the 29% miss rate over 34 games is honest mid-tier work. He's a 2026 walk year in Tampa — the profile (15% elite) is good enough to keep starting, volatile enough that the contract decision is genuinely open.
reports.Sam Darnold: Darnold regressed hard from the Minnesota magic — 18.8 to 14.7 PPG with 35% misses and a 9% elite rate. Seattle has him committed through 2027, but the 2025 tape looked like the older Darnold, not the 2024 one.
reports.C.J. Stroud: Stroud's profile has cratered: 61% misses with a 3% elite rate across 31 games, and PPG stuck in the 13-15 range since the rookie peak of 18.7. The 0.11 EPA/play says the play isn't broken, but two straight years of fantasy irrelevance is a trend, not a slump.
reports.Jaxson Dart: Dart arrived fast — 17.6 PPG as a rookie with a 71% hit rate and 14% elite across 14 starts. The sample is small and the EPA (0.06) modest, but franchise-QB capital locked through 2029 plus immediate production is exactly the early-career shape you want.
reports.Cam Ward: Ward's rookie year was a grind: 11.4 PPG, 76% misses, zero elite games in 17 starts, with a -0.17 EPA/play. The draft capital and Tennessee's commitment through 2029 buy him time, but the year-one tape offers almost nothing to project from yet.
reports.Bryce Young: Young has stabilized without ascending — back-to-back ~14.5 PPG seasons, EPA/play improved from -0.23 to flat, but 60% misses across 30 games. Carolina's committed through 2027; the profile says competent starter, and fantasy needs more than that.
reports.Matthew Stafford: Stafford turned in a 21.1 PPG season at 38 with a 0.17 EPA/play — genuinely excellent football. But 45% misses, a 2026 walk year, and the age cliff make this a pure win-now rental; there is no long game here.
reports.Kyler Murray: Murray's slide continued — 16.2 PPG, third straight year of decline — though the 64% hit rate and 14% elite across 22 games show the weekly output isn't dead. Now in Minnesota on a one-year bridge: a prove-it season at 28.6 with real route back to relevance if the situation clicks.
reports.Joe Burrow: When Burrow plays, he hits — 76% hit rate, 24% elite, with only 24% misses across 25 games. The 25-game sample is the story: availability keeps interrupting elite production (15.3→22.5→17.4 PPG whiplash). Locked through 2029 at 29.3, he's a top-five profile with an injury asterisk.
reports.Tua Tagovailoa: Tua's 2025 fell apart — 12.5 PPG, 52% misses, 4% elite across 25 games — and now he's in Atlanta through 2028 trying to reboot. The 0.19 EPA/play from the lost year suggests the efficiency didn't fully die, but the weekly fantasy output did.
reports.Geno Smith: Geno's profile collapsed: 12.7 PPG, 63% misses, zero elite games in 32 starts, with EPA/play sinking to -0.14. At 35 on a one-year stopgap with the Jets, this is end-of-the-line maintenance, not an asset.
reports.J.J. McCarthy: McCarthy's debut was a coin flip — literally 50/50 hit-miss across 10 starts at 13.7 PPG, zero elite games, -0.15 EPA/play. Locked through 2028, the investment thesis is entirely about year two; year one proved only that he can take the field.
reports.Daniel Jones: Jones resurrected himself in Indianapolis — 18.0 PPG, a 61% hit rate, and a 0.17 EPA/play that's the best of his career, now paid through 2027 on the $88M transition extension. The 4% elite rate keeps the ceiling honest: this is a high-floor QB2 story, not a late breakout.
reports.Tyler Shough: Shough held the job — 14.9 PPG, 64% hit rate across 11 starts, zero elite games. The cheap deal through 2028 and full runway in New Orleans make him a plausible bridge-to-something, but the tape is placeholder quality so far.
Penix improved from 9.4 to 13.7 PPG but the profile is still bleak — 64% misses, zero elite games in 14 starts — and Atlanta importing Tua through 2028 says everything about the organization's confidence. The arm talent thesis needs a new home or a collapse in front of him.
reports.Shedeur Sanders: Sanders barely played — 8 starts, 11.9 PPG, 88% misses — though the lone elite game (13% of a tiny sample) flashed something. Cheap deal through 2028 in Cleveland; this is a lottery ticket priced on name recognition, not production.
reports.Mason Rudolph: Rudolph's 2025 was 3.4 PPG across spot duty with 77% career-window misses. Pittsburgh moves on after 2026 — there's no fantasy case here beyond emergency streaming.
reports.Aaron Rodgers: Rodgers at 42.3 posted 14.2 PPG with 48% misses and a 3% elite rate — professional-grade decline management. The one-year Pittsburgh bridge is the final chapter; nothing here projects forward.
reports.Malik Willis: Willis remains a fascinating box-score anomaly — a 0.49 EPA/play in limited 2025 work, 12.8 PPG, but 82% misses across 11 appearances. Miami committed through 2028, and the efficiency-in-relief pattern is real enough to keep watching without paying for.
reports.Jacoby Brissett: Brissett delivered 16.8 PPG in 2025 relief work with a 50% hit rate — vintage capable-backup output. He's a veteran bridge in Arizona with no dynasty equity beyond emergency relevance.
reports.Mac Jones: Jones didn't take a meaningful 2025 snap and the prior profile (8-11 PPG) was below the line when he did. Backup QB on the depth chart and in dynasty terms.
reports.Joe Flacco: Flacco at 41.8 is roster insurance in Cincinnati, nothing more. The 2023 magic is three years old; there's no profile left to project.
reports.Justin Fields: Fields' 2025 in Kansas City fell to 15.4 PPG with 53% misses, though the 16% elite rate shows the rushing spike weeks still happen. A backup behind Mahomes with a profile built entirely on what-if — the window for the breakout narrative has functionally closed.
reports.Kirk Cousins: Cousins didn't start in 2025 and turns 38 this season. Las Vegas signed Mendoza at #1 overall; Cousins is the bridge's bridge.
reports.Fernando Mendoza: The #1 overall pick, locked through 2029, with Cousins as a short bridge in front of him. No NFL data yet — the asset is pure draft capital and the Raiders' total commitment, which is exactly what you want from a rookie QB hold.
reports.Ty Simpson: First-round capital (#13) through 2029 but currently buried on the Rams' depth chart behind Stafford's last act. A patience play: the capital says future starter, the situation says not yet.
reports.Carson Beck: Third-round flier (#65) backing up in Arizona. Developmental hold with modest capital — roster him in deep superflex only.
reports.Drew Allar: Third-round pick (#76) walking into Pittsburgh's open QB competition. The path to starts is more real than the capital suggests — worth a late-round superflex stash.
reports.Garrett Nussmeier: Seventh-round pick parked behind Mahomes. Practice-squad-tier dynasty asset; the situation is the only interesting part.
reports.Cade Klubnik: Fourth-round developmental pick with the Jets behind Geno Smith's one-year deal. The depth chart could open fast — a thin but live superflex lottery ticket.
reports.Taylen Green: Sixth-round developmental QB in Cleveland. Roster-clogger in all but the deepest formats.
reports.Bijan Robinson: Bijan is the position's gold standard right now: 82% hit rate, 35% elite, just 18% misses across a full 34 games, with PPG steady at 19.5. At 24.1 on a cheap deal through 2027 with 4.7 receptions a game, this is the complete RB profile — the only question is what the second contract costs Atlanta.
reports.Jahmyr Gibbs: Gibbs misses just 15% of starts with a 32% elite rate over 34 games — back-to-back 19+ PPG seasons at age 24. Still on the rookie deal through 2027 with 4.5 catches a game; the profile and the trajectory both point the same direction, and that direction is up.
reports.James Cook: Cook has improved PPG three straight years (12.4→15.7→16.8) and Buffalo paid him through 2029 — role security in ink. The 30% miss rate is the tax on a 1.9 rec/game profile that leans touchdown-dependent; the 24% elite rate says the spike weeks are real.
reports.Jonathan Taylor: Taylor bounced back to 20.0 PPG with an 81% hit rate and 32% elite across 31 games — vintage workhorse output at 27.2. The 2026 walk year is the whole story: Indy extends him or this becomes a sell-window decision mid-season.
reports.Derrick Henry: Henry at 32.2 still hit in 88% of games — the highest floor in the RB room — though PPG slipped to 16.0 and the elite rate (26%) came down from the 2024 peak. Two years left on the deal; the profile is aging gracefully, but it is aging.
reports.Kyren Williams: Kyren's floor is elite — 82% hit rate, 18% misses — but the ceiling has evaporated: a 6% elite rate and three straight years of PPG decline (19.9→15.9→14.4). Locked through 2028, he's the safest boring asset at the position.
reports.Saquon Barkley: Saquon's 2025 crashed to 13.4 PPG from the historic 21.2, with misses jumping to 31%. The 25% elite rate says the explosive weeks didn't vanish, but at 29.1 with two years left, the sell-window framing isn't pessimism — it's the actuarial table.
reports.Christian McCaffrey: CMC delivered a 21.5 PPG season at nearly 30, with a 38% elite rate and 6.0 catches a game when active — the per-game profile is still top-three. The 21-game two-year sample is the entire risk: you're buying games played, not games dominated.
reports.Chase Brown: Brown has stacked consecutive ~14.5 PPG seasons with a 73% hit rate and 4.1 receptions a game. The 2026 expiring deal is the swing factor — an extension confirms the RB1 role; anything else reopens the committee question.
reports.Travis Etienne: Etienne rebounded to 13.9 PPG and New Orleans signed him as the RB1 through 2029, but the profile is a coin flip — 50% misses, 3% elite across 32 games. Paid like a starter, producing like a flex.
reports.Javonte Williams: Javonte's Dallas reboot worked — a career-best 14.1 PPG with EPA finally positive — and he's locked through 2028. The 52% miss rate tempers it: this is a volume-secured RB2, not a return to the pre-injury trajectory.
reports.Josh Jacobs: Jacobs is one of the quietest floors in football: 88% hit rate, only 13% misses across 32 games. PPG settled at 14.6 and the 16% elite rate caps the upside, but locked through 2027 in Green Bay, he's a weekly answer at the position.
reports.Ashton Jeanty: Jeanty's rookie year was bumpier than the draft capital promised — 12.8 PPG, 47% misses — but the 12% elite rate and 3.2 catches a game show the three-down toolkit. Locked through 2029 as Vegas's franchise piece; year two is the real referendum.
reports.D'Andre Swift: Swift hit a career-best 13.2 PPG, but 45% misses and a 6% elite rate is the same flex-tier profile he's always carried. A 2026 walk year in Chicago makes him a fade-at-the-deadline candidate.
reports.Jaylen Warren: Warren got the lead-back chance and produced 12.3 PPG with 55% misses — committee output even with the committee thinned. Two years with Dowdle alongside; the profile says useful, never weekly-trustworthy.
reports.Rico Dowdle: Dowdle has stacked two ~11.5 PPG seasons with a 45% hit rate — replacement-plus production now contracted through 2027 in Pittsburgh's backfield split. A depth piece with standalone value only in deep formats.
reports.Breece Hall: The Jets paid Hall through 2028 ($29M guaranteed) even as his PPG slid three straight years (14.9→13.3→11.9) with 44% misses. The contract buys him every opportunity to reverse the trend; the on-field profile hasn't earned it yet.
reports.TreVeyon Henderson: Henderson's rookie sample is the definition of boom-bust: 53% misses but an 18% elite rate across 17 games. Cheap through 2028 in New England — the spike weeks are the asset, and they're real.
reports.Kenneth Gainwell: Gainwell parlayed 4.3 catches a game into a 10.9 PPG season and a two-year Tampa deal, but 79% misses tells the truth — this is a passing-down specialist, not a fantasy starter.
reports.RJ Harvey: Harvey split his rookie year evenly — 47% hits, 10.8 PPG, 2.8 catches a game — with the cheap-deal-through-2028 runway to grow into more. Denver's backfield remains the variable; the receiving chops are the path.
reports.Kenneth Walker III: Kansas City made a major investment (3yr/$43M) in a back coming off his worst season — 10.4 PPG, 43% misses, three straight years of decline. The contract says feature role; the profile says the burst that justified it is in question.
reports.Zach Charbonnet: Charbonnet inched up to 10.7 PPG but the 52% miss rate and expiring 2026 deal leave him a volume-dependent flex in a murky Seattle backfield. Watch the contract, not the tape.
reports.Tony Pollard: Pollard's profile reads done: 58% misses, 3% elite, three years of decline to 10.0 PPG, and a confirmed final season in Tennessee. The fading tag is earned — this is end-stage RB asset management.
reports.Rhamondre Stevenson: Stevenson is locked through 2028 but has hovered at 10.6-11.6 PPG for three years with 52% misses. New England's commitment keeps him startable in name; the per-game output is flex-tier and flat.
reports.Quinshon Judkins: Judkins flashed in a 14-game rookie sample — 11.2 PPG, though 57% missed the line. Cheap through 2028 with Cleveland's backfield his to take; the year-one efficiency (-0.11 EPA) needs the offense around him to improve.
reports.David Montgomery: Montgomery's 2025 fell to 9.1 PPG with the Houston move, and the 6% elite rate makes him touchdown-or-bust. Committed through 2027 as the veteran half of a committee — fine floor, no story.
reports.Woody Marks: Marks logged 8.7 PPG as a rookie with 69% misses — a depth-chart survivor on a cheap four-year deal. Houston's backfield is crowded; he's a hold only in deep formats.
reports.Kyle Monangai: Monangai's rookie year produced 8.1 PPG, zero elite games, and a 65% miss rate. The cheap Chicago deal through 2028 keeps the door cracked, but the profile is pure depth.
reports.Kareem Hunt: Hunt at 30 is a free agent with an 8.0 PPG season and 57% misses behind him. Veteran insurance, no dynasty equity.
reports.Jacory Croskey-Merritt: Croskey-Merritt got 17 rookie games and produced 8.0 PPG with 71% misses. Cheap Washington deal, ascending-age profile, but nothing in the output yet demands a roster spot outside deep leagues.
reports.Bucky Irving: Irving's 70% hit rate across 27 games is quietly excellent for a back this cheap, with PPG holding around 12-13. Through 2027 on the rookie deal — the 2025 EPA dip (-0.21) and 30% miss rate are the watch items, but the foundation is real.
reports.Rachaad White: White's three-year slide (13.9→10.9→7.2 PPG) with 67% misses tells a complete story. The 2.4 receptions a game keep him on rosters; nothing else does.
reports.Jordan Mason: Mason's Minnesota stint produced 7.6 PPG with 68% misses, and the deal expires in 2026. Short runway, shorter profile.
reports.Omarion Hampton: Hampton's nine-game rookie sample was promising — 13.3 PPG, 67% hit rate — before the season was cut short. Locked through 2029 with the Chargers' backfield his; the per-game output already looks like an every-week starter when healthy.
reports.Blake Corum: Corum has 16% hits across two seasons and 7.0 PPG in the bigger sample year. The Rams' rookie deal runs through 2027; the profile is a handcuff's handcuff.
reports.Cam Skattebo: Skattebo's eight-game rookie flash — 14.5 PPG, 75% hit rate — is one of the more interesting small samples at the position. Cheap through 2028 as the Giants' developing RB1; if the workload holds, the profile is already startable.
reports.J.K. Dobbins: Dobbins gave Denver 11.0 PPG with a 57% hit rate — competent committee work, contracted through 2027. The 4% elite rate is the ceiling truth: the explosive version of Dobbins is several injuries ago.
reports.Chuba Hubbard: Hubbard crashed from 14.7 to 7.4 PPG with 53% misses, and the contract through 2028 now reads like an anchor rather than security. The fading tag plus a declining role makes him a sell-on-name candidate.
reports.Bhayshul Tuten: Tuten's rookie year barely registered — 5.6 PPG, 87% misses. The cheap Jacksonville deal through 2028 buys development time; there's nothing in the output yet.
reports.Aaron Jones: Jones at 31.3 posted 8.7 PPG with a fading tag and a 2026 walk year — the graceful-decline phase is complete. The 66% hit rate is residual professionalism, not an asset.
reports.Tyjae Spears: Spears never claimed the open Tennessee role — 6.9 PPG, 76% misses — and the deal expires in 2026. Cheap handcuff, closing window.
reports.Alvin Kamara: Kamara's 2025 collapse (7.7 PPG from 16.5, -0.22 EPA) in his walk year reads terminal. The 8% elite rate is muscle memory; the profile says the end arrived fast.
reports.De'Von Achane: Achane misses just 18% of starts with an 18% elite rate, and Miami paid him — 4yr/$64M signed in May. The 2025 dip to 12.8 PPG from 18.7 is the one wrinkle, but at 24.0 with 3.9 catches a game and a rising tag, this is a top-six profile with contract security.
reports.Ollie Gordon: Gordon's rookie year produced 3.0 PPG in scattered work — 82% misses. Cheap Miami deal, deep-roster stash only.
reports.Isaac Guerendo: Guerendo didn't log a qualifying 2025 sample, and the prior year was 5.4 PPG depth work. Handcuff equity in San Francisco's system, nothing standalone.
reports.Trey Benson: Benson has 12% hits across two seasons with under 6 PPG. The Arizona rookie deal runs through 2027; the opportunity hasn't come and the output hasn't argued for it.
reports.Dylan Sampson: Sampson's rookie line — 4.7 PPG, 87% misses, -0.26 EPA — is a developmental redshirt in box-score form. Cheap through 2028 in Cleveland's crowded backfield.
reports.Jaylen Wright: Wright has an 8% hit rate across two years and 4.2 PPG in 2025. Miami depth with a closing developmental window.
reports.Braelon Allen: Allen's two-year profile is 10% hits and a brutal -0.43 EPA in 2025. Young (22.2) and cheap through 2027, but the Jets' usage says depth, and the output agrees.
reports.James Conner: Conner gave Arizona a 74% hit rate when active but managed only 19 games over two years, and 2025 fell to 7.3 PPG. Walk year at 30.2 — the profile is done compounding.
reports.Isiah Pacheco: Pacheco's slide (13.7→7.3→6.0 PPG, 75% misses) through injury and a Detroit move leaves little to project. Name value exceeds profile value — that gap is sell material.
reports.Jonathon Brooks: Brooks has three career games and a 100% miss rate while recovering. The 2027 rookie deal and draft pedigree are the entire asset; this is a pure health lottery ticket.
reports.Tank Bigsby: Bigsby improved to 8.1 PPG with a 0.12 EPA in 2025, but 67% misses keeps him in the depth tier. Flashes without a role.
reports.Alexander Mattison: Mattison is a 26.7-year-old free agent coming off 4.8 PPG. No contract, no role, no case.
reports.Audric Estime: Estime has 16% hits and 6.3 PPG across two Denver seasons. Deep-league depth.
reports.MarShawn Lloyd: Lloyd has one career qualifying game. The athletic profile that made him a pick remains theoretical — droppable outside the deepest formats.
reports.Kimani Vidal: Vidal's 26% hit rate and 5.2 PPG are Chargers depth-chart filler. Watch-list only.
reports.Tyrone Tracy: Tracy has stacked two ~9.5 PPG seasons with 2.1 catches a game — fringe-flex output on a cheap deal through 2027. The Giants adding Skattebo caps the ceiling; the floor is roster-worthy in deeper leagues.
reports.Brian Robinson: Robinson's three-year fade (12.0→10.7→6.9 PPG, 65% misses) has run its course in Washington. Veteran depth.
reports.George Holani: Holani has seven career games, all misses. Camp body.
reports.Keaton Mitchell: Mitchell's 2023 lightning (16.4 PPG) is two injuries and a team change ago — 94% misses since. The speed is real; the opportunity isn't.
reports.Chris Rodriguez: Rodriguez quietly hit 12.9 PPG in limited 2025 work, but the two-year profile is 75% misses. A name to watch if Jacksonville's depth chart breaks, not before.
reports.Sean Tucker: Tucker posted 13.5 PPG in scattered 2025 opportunity, but 89% career misses define the role. Spot-start upside, no security.
reports.Ray Davis: Davis fell back to 6.6 PPG behind Cook's extension — 79% misses across a full 34 games. Buffalo handcuff, full stop.
reports.Tyler Allgeier: Allgeier landed in Arizona and produced 12.9 PPG in his share, but the 79% miss rate reflects the committee reality. Reliable veteran depth with a known ceiling.
reports.Jeremiyah Love: The #3 overall pick, locked through 2029 — the highest-capital RB asset in the class with no NFL tape yet. Price reflects the pedigree; the profile starts writing itself in September.
reports.Jadarian Price: First-round capital (#32) through 2029 in Seattle's open backfield. The capital-to-opportunity ratio is among the best in the rookie class.
reports.Kaelon Black: Third-round pick (#90) in San Francisco's system, which has a history of making Day 3 backs relevant. Developmental stash with a real scheme tailwind.
reports.Nicholas Singleton: Fifth-round pick parked behind Tennessee's offense rebuild. Capital and situation both modest — deep-roster flier.
reports.Emmett Johnson: Fifth-round pick in the Mahomes offense, which inflates every skill player's theoretical ceiling. Stash-tier with a better-than-capital situation.
reports.Jonah Coleman: Fourth-round pick in a Sean Payton system that historically feeds running backs. The fit is the asset — worth a late stash on scheme alone.
Fourth-round pick in the Raiders' new Mendoza-era backfield. Depth with a path if Jeanty's workload needs relief.
reports.Adam Randall: Fifth-round converted-WR athlete in Baltimore. Traits flier, nothing more yet.
reports.Kaytron Allen: Sixth-round depth in Washington. Deepest-league stash only.
reports.Demond Claiborne: Sixth-round depth in Minnesota. Watch-list material.
reports.Puka Nacua: Nacua's ascent hasn't paused: 19.4 PPG on 7.6 catches a game, a 3.73 YPRR that leads the position, and just 15% misses with 30% elite. The 2026 expiring contract is the only cloud — the Rams extending him is the most predictable transaction in football, but until ink dries it's the risk.
reports.Jaxon Smith-Njigba: JSN's trajectory is a straight line up: 7.0→11.9→17.7 PPG with YPRR doubling to 3.67, now locked through 2031 on the franchise deal. The 29% miss rate from the leaner early years will keep shrinking as the 2025 version becomes the baseline.
reports.Amon-Ra St. Brown: St. Brown is the position's metronome — four straight years of 2.3+ YPRR, 15+ PPG, 6.9 catches a game, locked through 2028. The 21% elite rate undersells how rarely he loses you a week; 29% misses is the only blemish on an otherwise complete profile.
reports.George Pickens: Pickens broke out in Dallas — a career-best 14.4 PPG with a 2.37 YPRR and rising tag — but the 2026 walk year turns the breakout into a negotiation. The 42% miss rate is the volatility tax; the talent finally has the target volume to justify the price.
reports.Chris Olave: Olave bounced back to 13.7 PPG on 5.9 catches a game, quieting the durability narrative for a season. Four straight years of ~2.0+ YPRR says the per-route talent never left; the expiring cheap deal makes 2026 a contract-and-health referendum.
reports.Zay Flowers: Flowers keeps climbing — 11.8 PPG with a 2.55 YPRR that ranks among the position's best, and a rising tag at 25.5. The 50% miss rate exposes the offense's distribution problem more than his; through 2027 with an extension looming, the underlying profile is better than the box scores.
reports.Davante Adams: Adams at 33.2 still produced 13.8 PPG with a 21% elite rate — the craft hasn't aged even as the calendar has. A 2026 walk year on the final deal: pure win-now value with a clean expiration date.
reports.Nico Collins: Collins held a 67% hit rate but slipped to 12.7 PPG, with YPRR easing from 3.07 to 2.39 — still excellent, no longer otherworldly. Locked through 2027 as Houston's WR1; the 33% miss rate tracks the offense's inconsistency more than his.
reports.Jameson Williams: Williams earned the major extension through 2029, and the 22% elite rate shows why — when it connects, it's a week-winner. The 44% miss rate is the other half of the deal: a boom-bust profile now paid like a cornerstone.
reports.Courtland Sutton: Sutton at 30.4 remains the definition of steady — three straight years of 10-12 PPG, a 59% hit rate, locked through 2029. The 9% elite rate caps the appeal; this is a WR3 floor with name-brand pricing.
reports.Tee Higgins: Higgins produced 12.1 PPG with a 19% elite rate when active, but 27 games over two years keeps availability in the conversation. Locked through 2028 alongside Chase — the role is secure, the body is the variable.
reports.A.J. Brown: Brown's 12.1 PPG was his quietest in years, and the YPRR slide from 3.01 to 2.13 is worth watching at 28.7. The 21% elite rate and long-term deal keep the profile firmly WR1 — but 2025 was the first tape where the dominance flickered.
reports.Tetairoa McMillan: McMillan's rookie year delivered 10.4 PPG on 4.1 catches a game with a 1.88 YPRR — solid foundation, no fireworks (6% elite). Locked through 2028 as Carolina's WR1; the profile is ascending on schedule.
reports.Wan'Dale Robinson: Robinson's 5.4 catches a game and improving PPG (10.7) hide a 73% miss rate — volume without verticality. Locked cheap through 2029; the YPRR jump to 1.9 in 2025 is the first hint of a deeper role.
reports.Drake London: London's two-year run — 13.6 then 14.0 PPG, with a 2.36 YPRR — confirms the target-monster profile, but the 2026 walk year makes Atlanta's next move the entire asset question. The 45% miss rate is real: he wins on accumulation weeks and disappears on script-broken ones.
reports.Emeka Egbuka: Egbuka's rookie sample ran hot and cold — 9.7 PPG with an 18% elite rate against 65% misses. Locked through 2029 with Tampa's WR room aging around him; the spike weeks suggest the role grows into the talent.
reports.Ja'Marr Chase: Chase remains the WR1 by market and mostly by profile — a 33% elite rate, 7.3 catches a game, four straight years above 2.1 YPRR. The 2025 dip to 15.7 PPG from 20.0 tracks Burrow's missed time more than any decline; locked through 2029, he's the position's safest blue chip.
reports.CeeDee Lamb: Lamb's three-year PPG slide (19.7→14.2→11.7) is the quiet alarm at WR — the 2.4 YPRR says the talent is intact, so the indictment lands on role and offense. Locked through 2029 at 26.9; this is a buy-the-dip or watch-the-decline fork, and 2026 decides which.
reports.DeVonta Smith: Smith faded to 9.6 PPG with a 47% miss rate — third banana in a run-first offense is a hard fantasy job. The 1.96 YPRR confirms the skill; the fading tag reflects the role. Locked through 2028.
Pittman's profile has flatlined: 9.6 PPG, 55% misses, a 3% elite rate despite 4.7 catches a game. Paid through 2029 — the contract outruns the ceiling at this point.
reports.Jaylen Waddle: Waddle's Denver move came with a big investment through 2028, but the profile sagged to 10.1 PPG with 65% misses. The 2.28 YPRR rebound is the bull case — the per-route player is better than the 2025 usage showed.
reports.Alec Pierce: Pierce earned the major extension as Indy's WR1 with a 2.19 YPRR and improving PPG (10.7), but the 58% miss rate exposes the deep-ball-dependent profile. Paid through 2029 — week-winning ceiling, coin-flip floor.
reports.Justin Jefferson: Jefferson's 9.4 PPG and 1.92 YPRR were career worsts by wide margins — the QB carousel in Minnesota finally showed up in his line. The track record (2.5+ YPRR for years prior) makes 2025 read as situation, not decline, at 26.8. Locked through 2028; this is the highest-pedigree discount on the board.
reports.DK Metcalf: Metcalf has settled into a 10.5 PPG, 50/50 hit-miss existence — the 2.06 YPRR says the athlete remains, the 7% elite rate says the offense doesn't weaponize it. Locked through 2029 in Pittsburgh.
reports.Parker Washington: Washington tripled his output to 9.7 PPG with a 2.1 YPRR breakout in 2025, and the expiring deal makes him a cheap-extension candidate. The 73% career miss rate is mostly old-role residue — the 2025 version is the one to price.
reports.Ladd McConkey: McConkey's sophomore dip — 12.5 to 9.2 PPG, YPRR nearly halved to 1.42 — earned the fading tag honestly. Still cheap through 2027 at 24.4; the rookie-year tape argues for patience, the 2025 tape argues for concern. Genuine fork.
reports.D.J. Moore: Moore's Buffalo move produced 8.5 PPG with 62% misses and a 1.25 YPRR — career lows across the board. The 15% elite rate is the residue of talent; the role says WR3 in a run-leaning offense, and the contract through 2029 doesn't change the usage.
reports.Troy Franklin: Franklin grew into 8.5 PPG with a 1.44 YPRR in year two — modest, real progress. Cheap through 2027; he's a developmental hold whose 79% miss rate needs a role change to move.
reports.Jakobi Meyers: Meyers in Jacksonville produced 8.6 PPG with a 58% miss rate and 3% elite — reliable-hands volume without ceiling. Locked through 2028 as a possession WR2; the profile is fully priced in.
reports.Romeo Doubs: Doubs parlayed three identical ~8.5 PPG seasons into New England's WR1 money through 2029. The 66% miss rate hasn't moved in three years — the contract bet is that role elevation changes a profile that's never changed.
reports.Quentin Johnston: Johnston quietly improved again — 10.4 PPG, 52% hits, a third straight year of growth. Through 2027; the 10% elite rate and first-round pedigree keep the late-breakout case technically alive.
reports.Khalil Shakir: Shakir's 4.2 catches a game and 1.74 YPRR mask a 65% miss rate with zero elite games — high-floor, no-ceiling in its purest form. Locked through 2029 in Buffalo as a fine real-life player and a frustrating fantasy one.
reports.Rome Odunze: Odunze's rising tag is earned — 10.3 PPG in year two with the Williams connection developing. The 69% miss rate says the breakout is partial; locked through 2028, the pedigree-plus-trajectory combination is exactly what you stash before year three.
reports.Rashee Rice: Rice's per-game profile is electric — 15.5 PPG, 33% elite rate, 3.16 YPRR in 2024 — but it spans only 12 recent games. The expiring 2026 deal and extension chatter add contract noise to injury noise; when he plays, he's a top-ten WR, full stop.
reports.Christian Watson: Watson rebounded to 11.5 PPG with a 2.55 YPRR — the per-route numbers have always been excellent — but 68% career misses and a 2026 walk year on an incentive deal keep this a high-variance flier.
Thomas crashed from 14.1 to 8.2 PPG and a 1.54 YPRR — the sophomore slump was total, and the fading tag reflects it. Cheap through 2028 at 23.4 with a rookie year that proved the ceiling; this is the classic buy-the-frustration window if you believe 2025 was offense-wide.
reports.Jordan Addison: Addison slid to 8.2 PPG with 59% misses as Minnesota's offense sputtered. Cheap through 2027 — the 2023-24 production says startable WR3; the situation has to stabilize first.
reports.Luther Burden: Burden barely got on the field — 7.0 PPG, 80% misses — but the 2.71 YPRR in his rookie sample is a screaming efficiency signal. Rising tag, cheap through 2028: this is the buy-before-the-role-arrives window.
reports.Elic Ayomanor: Ayomanor's rookie year was quiet — 6.0 PPG, zero elite games, a 1.06 YPRR. Cheap deal, developmental timeline; needs a real role before the profile means anything.
reports.Terry McLaurin: McLaurin at 30.5 still posted a 2.26 YPRR — the skill aging better than the fantasy line (9.5 PPG). The fading tag is about trajectory, not talent; locked through 2028 as a win-now piece with a closing window.
reports.Tre Harris: Harris's rookie year was a zero — 3.2 PPG, no hits in 17 games. Whatever the draft pedigree promised, year one delivered none of it; deep-stash only.
reports.Jayden Higgins: Higgins managed 6.4 PPG with 76% misses as a rookie in Houston's crowded room. Locked through 2028; needs attrition above him to matter.
reports.Matthew Golden: Golden's rookie year was nearly invisible — 4.0 PPG, 93% misses — despite the fully guaranteed deal through 2029. The capital says be patient; the tape says there's nothing to evaluate yet.
reports.Ricky Pearsall: Pearsall has stacked two identical 7.1 PPG seasons with a 1.9 YPRR uptick in 2025. San Francisco committed through 2028 — ascending, slowly, in an offense that creates roles.
reports.Mike Evans: Evans at 32.6 hit a 23% elite rate when active — the contested-catch dominance hasn't aged — but 8.7 PPG across 22 games and a move to San Francisco as the WR2 reframe him as a touchdown-dependent veteran. Three-year deal; win-now only.
Two years in, Harrison's profile reads ordinary: 8.9 PPG, 55% misses, a 1.61 YPRR that hasn't budged, and now a fading tag. The pedigree keeps the price up; the production says the market is still paying for the draft slot. Locked through 2028 — the gap between name and numbers is the trade.
reports.Garrett Wilson: Wilson holds an 11.6 PPG line with a 13% elite rate despite the Jets' QB chaos — the 1.78 YPRR is fine, never spectacular. Locked long-term through 2030; the fading tag reflects offense fatigue more than skill decline, but four years of waiting for the situation is a real cost.
reports.Xavier Worthy: Worthy regressed to 6.4 PPG with zero elite games — the speed hasn't translated to a weekly role even in the Mahomes offense. Cheap through 2028; the theoretical ceiling keeps shrinking against the actual usage.
reports.Keon Coleman: Coleman's two years read 7.5 then 6.4 PPG with 77% misses — the big-frame upside remains entirely projection. Cheap through 2027 in Buffalo's WR-needy offense; the opportunity argument is better than the production argument.
reports.Josh Downs: Downs slipped to 6.7 PPG as Indy's offense consolidated elsewhere, but the 2024 tape (10.5 PPG, 2.19 YPRR) showed a real player. Expiring 2026 — the extension decision will define the asset.
reports.Travis Hunter: Hunter's seven-game rookie WR sample (7.1 PPG, one elite game) is too thin to grade — the two-way experiment ate the reps. Locked through 2028; the asset remains the athlete and the novelty, not yet the receiver.
reports.Brandon Aiyuk: Aiyuk sat out 2025 as a free agent after the lost 2024 — no current team, no current profile. The 2023 tape (3.02 YPRR) is the asset; everything else is a landing-spot lottery.
reports.Jalen Coker: Coker has hovered at ~6.8 PPG across two years with 73% misses, and the rookie deal expires in 2026. Deep-league depth on a short clock.
reports.Malik Nabers: Nabers' 19-game sample reads 14.6 then 12.0 PPG with a 21% elite rate and 2.07 YPRR — a true WR1 profile interrupted by availability. Locked through 2028 at 22.6; among the youngest players with proven target-dominance, the injury risk is the only discount mechanism.
reports.Deebo Samuel: Deebo is a 29.4-year-old free agent coming off 9.5 PPG — the YAC-monster profile needs both a team and a rebound. Name value only until there's a depth chart.
reports.Jauan Jennings: Jennings parlayed the 2024 breakout into free agency, then produced 9.7 PPG. Unsigned at 29 — a contested-catch role player waiting on a contract.
reports.Stefon Diggs: Diggs at 32 posted 9.9 PPG with zero elite games and remains unsigned. The 2.41 YPRR says the route-running survives; the market will price the age, not the craft.
reports.Jayden Reed: Reed's 10.4 PPG hides a 71% miss rate — a few enormous weeks, many empty ones — and the fading tag plus expiring 2026 deal compound the volatility. The 1.85 YPRR keeps him relevant; the usage keeps him unstartable.
reports.Calvin Ridley: Ridley's collapse to 5.5 PPG with 71% misses at 30.7 reads like the end arriving on schedule. Locked through 2027 in Tennessee — the contract outlasts the profile.
reports.Rashid Shaheed: Shaheed in Seattle produced 9.4 PPG with zero elite games — the deep-speed role aged into a possession line. Locked through 2028 as a WR3 with a vertical memory.
reports.Darnell Mooney: Mooney's 8.6 PPG and 0.96 YPRR in 2025 closed out his Atlanta run quietly. Veteran depth, free-agency pending.
reports.Rashod Bateman: Bateman inched to 9.8 PPG, but the 0.7 YPRR in 2025 is alarming — production without efficiency. Locked through 2029 in Baltimore's run-first ecosystem; the contract is longer than the case for him.
reports.Jalen McMillan: McMillan's 7.8 PPG rookie-adjacent year came with a 2.14 YPRR flash — there's a player here if Tampa's room thins. Watch-list with real underlying signal.
reports.Isaac TeSlaa: TeSlaa's rookie year was nearly empty — 6.4 PPG, 82% misses, 0.81 YPRR. Cheap through 2028 in a Detroit offense that could eventually feed him; pure patience play.
reports.Xavier Legette: Legette's two years read 5.8 and 7.2 PPG with zero elite games. Locked through 2028 — the first-round athleticism hasn't found a fantasy expression yet.
reports.Devaughn Vele: Vele improved to 7.6 PPG in Denver's rotation but missed 85% of the time. Deep-league depth.
reports.Chimere Dike: Dike's 17-game rookie year produced 5.8 PPG on 2.8 catches a game. Tennessee depth with return-game utility — fantasy-irrelevant for now.
reports.Jalen Nailor: Nailor parlayed a 9.6 PPG season into Vegas's WR1 contract through 2028, but the 85% career miss rate says the role outran the resume. The bet is all projection.
reports.Marvin Mims: Mims has three years of sub-7 PPG with an 81% miss rate — the 2024 YPRR spike (2.57) remains the lone tease. Gadget value, not lineup value.
reports.K.J. Osborn: Osborn is unsigned with eight 2025 games and zero hits. End of the dynasty line.
reports.Dontayvion Wicks: Wicks has never converted the YPRR flashes (2.04 in 2023) into production — 7.4 PPG, 87% misses. Green Bay depth on an expiring narrative.
reports.Jalen Tolbert: Tolbert fell from 13.5 to 5.6 PPG when Dallas's WR room got healthy — 80% misses tell the role story. Depth piece.
reports.Christian Kirk: Kirk's 5.8 PPG and 0.85 YPRR in 2025 closed his Jacksonville chapter at 28.8, now unsigned. The 2023 version is the resume; the 2025 version is the asset.
reports.Jack Bech: Bech's rookie year produced zero hits in 15 games (5.2 PPG). Vegas depth with Day 2 pedigree and nothing else yet.
reports.Jerry Jeudy: Jeudy regressed to 9.6 PPG with a 1.02 YPRR — the Cleveland QB situation swallowed whatever 2024 built. The 65% miss rate at 26.9 prices him as a WR4 with a better-tape memory.
reports.Cooper Kupp: Kupp at 33.1 produced 10.2 PPG in Seattle's WR2 role, with the YPRR (1.39) confirming the descent from greatness. The 3-year deal provides floor; nothing provides upside.
reports.Tez Johnson: Johnson's rookie year — 9.3 PPG in 16 games — outperformed the Day 3 capital. Tampa depth with slot-role potential worth monitoring.
reports.Chris Godwin: Godwin posted 12.5 PPG with a 19% elite rate in just 16 games — the per-game player remains legitimate. Unsigned at 30.4 coming off the injury; the landing spot determines whether this is a value or a memorial.
reports.Cedric Tillman: Tillman's 5.2 PPG and 83% miss rate in 2025 erased the 2024 flash. Cleveland depth, fading claim.
reports.Pat Bryant: Bryant's rookie year was quiet (7.8 PPG, 87% misses) in Denver's rotation. Developmental depth.
reports.Tre Tucker: Tucker keeps climbing quietly — 11.9 PPG on 3.4 catches a game — though the 79% career miss rate reflects the boom-bust usage. Vegas's WR room is thin enough that the role could keep growing.
reports.Kyle Williams: Williams managed 5.2 PPG as a rookie with 88% misses. New England depth chart roulette.
reports.Tank Dell: Dell didn't play in 2025 rehabbing the catastrophic injury — the 2023-24 profile (14+ PPG) was legitimately excellent. A walk-year wildcard whose entire value is a medical report.
reports.DeMario Douglas: Douglas's 8.4 PPG with 85% misses is slot-volume without fantasy conversion. The 2.02 YPRR hints at more; the usage never delivers it.
reports.Adonai Mitchell: Mitchell's two years read 91% misses with scattered deep shots. The athletic pedigree keeps him rostered in deep leagues; the production doesn't.
reports.Jaylin Noel: Noel logged 6.5 PPG as a rookie with a 94% miss rate. Houston depth — needs multiple injuries above him.
reports.Malik Washington: Washington produced 9.7 PPG on volume late in 2025, but 90% career misses define the profile. Miami depth with return value.
reports.Kayshon Boutte: Boutte has quietly stacked two ~12.5 PPG seasons with a 72% miss rate — productive in bursts, invisible otherwise. The New England role is real; the consistency isn't yet.
reports.Michael Wilson: Wilson's three steady years (13.0→11.2→10.7 PPG, 4.3 catches a game) make him Arizona's most dependable secondary option. The 6% elite rate caps it at WR3 — a useful, unexciting asset.
reports.Darius Slayton: Slayton's 87% miss rate across 30 games despite 10+ PPG seasons is the purest deep-threat-variance profile in the league. Giants depth.
reports.Marquise Brown: Brown flashed a 2.68 YPRR in 2024 but managed 18 games over two years and is now unsigned. Speed, fragility, free agency.
reports.Carnell Tate: The #4 overall pick, locked through 2029 as Tennessee's WR of the future. Elite capital, clean depth chart — the premium rookie WR profile.
reports.Jordyn Tyson: Top-ten capital (#8) through 2029 in New Orleans. The Olave pairing gives the offense two route-winners; Tyson's price is all pedigree and it's earned.
reports.Makai Lemon: First-round pick (#20) walking into Philadelphia's veteran-heavy room. The capital says future starter; the depth chart says year-two role.
reports.KC Concepcion: First-round pick (#24) in Cleveland's rebuilt receiver room. Capital-forward stash.
reports.Omar Cooper: First-rounder (#30) slotted as the Jets' WR2. Immediate-role rookie with real draft capital — one of the cleaner year-one paths in the class.
reports.De'Zhaun Stribling: Second-round pick (#33) who logged 7.2 PPG in early SF work. The system manufactures production; he's positioned to inherit some.
reports.Denzel Boston: Second-round pick (#39) as Cleveland's WR2 of the future. Standard Day 2 stash math.
reports.Germie Bernard: Second-round pick (#47) buried at WR3 in Pittsburgh. Capital without immediate path.
reports.Antonio Williams: Third-round pick (#71) in Washington's WR2 conversation. Modest capital, plausible role.
reports.Malachi Fields: Third-round pick (#74) in a thin Giants room. The depth chart is the argument.
reports.Zachariah Branch: Third-round pick (#79) behind Atlanta's established trio. Return-game value first, fantasy value later if ever.
reports.Ja'Kobi Lane: Third-round pick (#80) who saw brief rookie action (6.4 PPG sample). Baltimore's pass-game hierarchy limits the near-term ceiling.
reports.Chris Brazzell II: Third-round pick (#83) in Carolina's thin room — the path to targets is shorter than the capital suggests.
reports.Ted Hurst: Third-round pick (#84) in Tampa with Baker's full route tree above him. Stash-tier.
reports.Chris Bell: Third-round pick (#94) at WR3 in Miami. Deep-league flier.
reports.Caleb Douglas: Third-round pick (#75) in Miami's crowded young room. Capital says monitor; situation says wait.
reports.Zavion Thomas: Third-round pick (#89) in Chicago. Developmental depth.
reports.Barion Brown: Sixth-round speed in New Orleans behind Olave and Tyson. Watch-list only.
reports.Cyrus Allen: Fifth-round pick in the Mahomes orbit — the offense is the entire thesis.
reports.Reggie Virgil: Fifth-round depth in Arizona with a one-year-wonder college profile. Deepest leagues only.
reports.Kendrick Law: Fifth-round pick in Detroit's elite offense — thin path, good neighborhood.
reports.Kaden Wetjen: Fourth-round return specialist in Pittsburgh. Special-teams asset, not a fantasy one.
reports.Colbie Young: Fourth-round depth in Cincinnati. Deep stash at best.
reports.Deion Burks: Seventh-round pick who slid from Day 2 projections — Indy took the value. The talent-to-capital gap makes him the rare late-round name worth remembering.
reports.Bryce Lance: Fourth-round FCS product in New Orleans. Developmental flier.
reports.Skyler Bell: Fourth-round depth in Buffalo's offense — the ecosystem is the appeal.
reports.Brenen Thompson: Fourth-round pick with 4.26 speed in the Chargers' vertical offense. Pure traits lottery ticket.
reports.Trey McBride: McBride is the position's apex: just 9% misses with a 36% elite rate across 33 games, PPG climbing three straight years to 18.6 on 7.4 catches a game. Locked through 2029 — there is no cleaner profile at tight end, and the gap to the field is widening.
reports.Brock Bowers: Bowers misses only 17% of starts with a 28% elite rate — at 23.3, the youngest elite profile the position has produced in years. The 2025 dip to 14.7 PPG came with the YPRR easing to 1.7; still on the rookie deal through 2028, with the extension conversation already inevitable.
reports.George Kittle: Kittle at 32.4 posted a 46% elite rate — the highest at the position — with just 19% misses, and a rising tag to boot. The age is the entire discount; the 2.15 YPRR says the play hasn't declined yet. Locked through 2029 as the ultimate win-now TE.
reports.Sam LaPorta: LaPorta has settled into a good-not-great rhythm — 11.9 PPG, 64% hit rate, 16% elite — after the historic rookie year. At 25.2 the profile is stable and the Detroit offense remains the right ecosystem; the 36% miss rate is the gap between him and the top tier.
reports.Kyle Pitts: Pitts finally produced — a career-best 12.4 PPG on 5.2 catches a game — exactly in time for the 2026 walk year. The 53% miss rate keeps the skepticism honest; the talent-contract-timing collision makes him the most volatile asset at the position.
reports.Travis Kelce: Kelce at 36.4 still hits 70% of the time with a 21% elite rate — the decline is graceful, but PPG has slid three straight years (14.6→12.2→11.4). A walk year he'll likely extend through; the asset is the remaining runway, measured in months.
reports.Tyler Warren: Warren's rookie year delivered 11.1 PPG with a 65% hit rate on 4.5 catches a game — immediate, legitimate TE1 production. Locked through 2029 with Indy fully committed; the trajectory at 23.8 is the asset.
Fannin's rookie sample is the quiet sensation at the position — 11.7 PPG, 75% hit rate, 19% elite at age 21.7, with a rising tag. Cheap through 2028; if the 16-game version is real, this is a top-five TE asset hiding at a discount.
reports.Dallas Goedert: Goedert produced a steady 12.3 PPG with a 68% hit rate, but the 2026 walk year at 31.2 puts a hard clock on the asset. Win-now value with an expiration date.
reports.Juwan Johnson: Johnson's 10.6 PPG on 4.5 catches a game is honest TE1 volume, locked through 2027. The 3% elite rate is the ceiling truth — streamable floor, never a difference-maker.
reports.Hunter Henry: Henry keeps grinding out ~10.5 PPG seasons with a 58% hit rate, now on a 2026 walk year at 31.3. Reliable, replaceable, expiring.
reports.Dalton Schultz: Schultz rebounded to 10.5 PPG on 4.8 catches a game with the walk year approaching in 2027. The 53% miss rate is the profile: usable volume, no ceiling.
reports.Colston Loveland: Loveland's rookie year flashed real ceiling — a 19% elite rate at age 21.9 — against a 56% miss rate that says the role is still forming. Locked through 2029 with first-class draft capital; the trajectory bet is the entire (substantial) price.
reports.AJ Barner: Barner nearly doubled his output to 8.7 PPG in year two on 3.1 catches a game. Cheap through 2027 and ascending — a deep-league TE2 with a plausible path to more.
reports.Oronde Gadsden: Gadsden's rookie sample — 8.8 PPG with a 13% elite rate — punched above the Day 3 capital. Cheap through 2028; one of the better young-TE lottery tickets.
reports.Mark Andrews: Andrews' slide is now three years long (13.5→11.1→7.7 PPG) with the YPRR collapsing to 1.21 and a fading tag. Locked through 2028 at 30.5 — the name still trades on the 2021-23 memory; the profile says sell into it.
reports.Theo Johnson: Johnson improved to 8.5 PPG in year two but remains a 63%-miss backup-tier asset. Affordable deal, modest case.
reports.Dalton Kincaid: Kincaid rebounded to 10.5 PPG with a 2.8 YPRR — quietly the best efficiency of his career — but the 52% miss rate and 4% elite rate keep the first-round promise unfulfilled. Through 2027, still competing for the role that was supposed to be his.
reports.Chigoziem Okonkwo: Okonkwo's move to Washington came with TE1 security through 2028, but three straight ~7 PPG seasons with a 3% elite rate define the ceiling. Volume insurance, not an asset.
reports.Cade Otton: Otton holds the Tampa TE1 job through 2028 with a 7.6 PPG profile and a 13% elite rate that flashes when the WR room thins. Streamer with occasional relevance.
reports.Brenton Strange: Strange nearly doubled to 9.8 PPG with a 1.74 YPRR in 2025 — real growth heading into an expiring 2026 deal. The extension decision is the tell on Jacksonville's belief.
reports.Tucker Kraft: Kraft's breakout was emphatic — 14.7 PPG with a 2.3 YPRR, PPG tripling over three years — and the expiring 2026 deal makes the extension a formality Green Bay can't avoid. The 44% miss rate is the lone asterisk on a top-five trajectory.
reports.Pat Freiermuth: Freiermuth regressed to 6.7 PPG with a fading tag, locked through 2028 in an offense that forgot him. The 2024 tape says capable; the trend says depth.
reports.T.J. Hockenson: Hockenson's three-year fade (14.6→8.7→7.5 PPG, YPRR down to 1.05) meets a 2026 walk year — the asset is dissolving in real time. The fading tag isn't a warning anymore; it's a description.
reports.Evan Engram: Engram fell to 6.4 PPG at 31.5 with a walk year ahead. The 2023 volume monster is gone; this is end-of-contract veteran depth.
reports.Gunnar Helm: Helm's rookie year produced 5.7 PPG with zero elite games, but Tennessee locked him in as the TE1 through 2028. The role security is the asset; the production needs to follow.
reports.Mason Taylor: Taylor's rookie sample was thin — 6.8 PPG in 13 games with a fading tag already attached. Locked through 2028 as the Jets' young TE1; the pedigree deserves a longer leash than the early returns.
reports.Isaiah Likely: Likely's Giants deal came with clear TE1 billing, then 2025 delivered 4.4 PPG with 70% misses — the worst output of his career in the biggest role of it. The fading tag is earned; the contract through 2028 forces patience the profile hasn't justified.
reports.Cole Kmet: Kmet's 76% miss rate across 33 games is among the worst at the position for a contracted starter. Locked through 2027 — volume without conversion, year after year.
reports.Jake Ferguson: Ferguson's 9.2 PPG on 4.8 catches a game is steady TE1 volume, locked through 2029 in Dallas. The 45% miss rate and 10% elite rate bracket him precisely: a fine starter who rarely swings a week.
reports.Dawson Knox: Knox at 30.2 is a 79%-miss depth piece in Buffalo. The 2021 touchdown season is ancient history.
reports.Noah Fant: Fant's three-year line is flat ~7 PPG with zero elite games. New Orleans depth.
reports.Jonnu Smith: Jonnu fell back to 7.4 PPG after the 2024 spike, with the YPRR halving to 0.77. The 18% elite rate is the residue of the Miami year — at 30, that's the memory being traded on.
reports.Charlie Kolar: Kolar's 90% miss rate makes the periodic YPRR spikes (2.91 in 2024) academic. Chargers depth.
reports.Elijah Higgins: Higgins has a 6% hit rate across two seasons. Roster filler.
reports.Greg Dulcich: Dulcich resurfaced with 10.2 PPG and a 2.29 YPRR in limited 2025 work — the receiving talent that made him a Day 2 pick still flickers. Unsigned; a landing-spot lottery with actual upside if someone gives him routes.
reports.David Njoku: Njoku's three-year consistency (12.4-13.5 PPG, 17% elite) makes him the best unsigned TE on the market at 28.8. Wherever he lands, the profile travels — a rare free-agency asset whose production is genuinely portable.
reports.Colby Parkinson: Parkinson improved to 8.6 PPG in the Rams' rotation. Streamer-tier with TD dependency.
reports.Terrance Ferguson: Ferguson's rookie year was quiet (7.8 PPG, 13 games) behind a crowded Rams room. Day 2 pedigree, developmental clock.
reports.Jake Tonges: Tonges produced 9.9 PPG in surprise 2025 volume, but the 81% miss rate frames it honestly: a waiver-wire story, not a profile.
reports.Michael Mayer: Mayer rebounded to 9.8 PPG but has missed 88% across the window. The Day 2 pedigree keeps him rostered; Bowers keeps him capped.
reports.JaTavion Sanders: Sanders regressed to 6.8 PPG in year two with zero elite games. Carolina depth with fading developmental shine.
reports.Ben Sinnott: Sinnott has a 3% hit rate across two seasons. Washington depth, full stop.
reports.Zach Ertz: Ertz somehow produced a 16.6 PPG season at 35.8 with a 60% hit rate — professional route-running outlasting the calendar entirely. There is no dynasty case, only a remarkable present tense.
reports.Kenyon Sadiq: The #16 overall pick, locked through 2029 as the Jets' TE1 — elite capital at a position where rookie-year production is rare. Price the pedigree, be patient with the profile.
reports.Eli Stowers: Second-round pick (#54) parked behind an established Eagle. Capital-forward stash with a delayed clock.
reports.Marlin Klein: Second-round pick (#59) drafted as a blocking-first TE in Houston. Capital without a fantasy thesis.
reports.Max Klare: Second-round pick (#61) in a crowded Rams TE room. Developmental hold.
reports.Sam Roush: Third-round blocking TE in Chicago. No fantasy case.
reports.Oscar Delp: Third-round blocking-first TE in New Orleans. Watch-list only.
reports.Will Kacmarek: Third-round blocking TE in Miami. Roster-clogger.
reports.Eli Raridon: Third-round pick (#95) in a thin New England TE room — the depth chart is the lone argument for relevance.
reports.Justin Joly: Fifth-round pick billed as Denver's most proven TE producer in the class. Late-round dart with a cleaner path than the capital suggests.
